figures.gloss: The holy fire kindled on the aukuras, the stone altar, at every Romuva rite — fed with offerings of grain, bread, salt and beer while participants sing; the perpetual fire of the old Baltic sanctuaries, which medieval chroniclers describe the Lithuanians tending, relit as the revival's central act.
figures.facet: Where the tier-honesty of the whole shard is decided: the fire the chroniclers saw was real and its cult attested, but no flame burned continuously from 1387 to 1967 — Romuva rekindles an attested rite across a break, and its own best spokesmen have framed it as restoration, not survival.
figures.provenance: Peter von Dusburg's Chronicon terrae Prussiae (1326) on the Romow sanctuary; Jerome of Prague's mission account of Lithuanian fire-veneration as preserved by Aeneas Sylvius Piccolomini (15th c.); modern rite in Jonas Trinkūnas (ed.), Of Gods and Holidays: The Baltic Heritage (1999).

figures.gloss: The vast corpus of Lithuanian folk hymns — including the polyphonic sutartinės — taken up as Romuva's working scripture: rites are not read but sung, and the songs carry Saulė, Perkūnas, Žemyna and Laima exactly as the collectors found them in village mouths.
figures.facet: A revival whose liturgy is identical with its evidence: the dainos are the very source layer from which scholars reconstruct Baltic religion, so in singing them Romuva performs its own bibliography — the rare case where the act of worship and the citation are one gesture.
figures.provenance: Liudvikas Rėza's Dainos oder Litthauische Volkslieder (1825) and the subsequent Lithuanian folklore collections; their ritual use in Trinkūnas 1999 and in Michael Strmiska's studies of Baltic pagan music revival.

figures.gloss: Romuva's ethical center: darna, the state of attunement — within oneself, the family, the community, the ancestors and the living land — that ritual, song and right conduct exist to maintain; the name Romuva itself is glossed from ramus, 'calm, peace'.
figures.facet: The cosmic-order joint filled by a revival: darna as articulated by Trinkūnas is a modern systematization drawn from folk material rather than an attested ancient doctrine — honest as the movement's synthesis, and structurally kin to the order-concepts of far older shards.
figures.provenance: Trinkūnas (ed.), Of Gods and Holidays: The Baltic Heritage (1999); discussion of Romuva theology in Aitamurto & Simpson (eds.), Modern Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Central and Eastern Europe (2013).

figures.gloss: The midsummer festival of dew and fire — wreaths on the water, fern-flower vigils, songs to the wedding sun — whose public celebration at the hillforts of Kernavė in 1967 was the founding act of the modern revival, staged as folklore under Soviet eyes because it could not be staged as religion.
figures.facet: The revival's tactical birth: Rasa 1967 shows a suppressed religion re-entering the world through the one door the state left open, ethnographic folklore — the festival was the vessel, and the faith stepped out of it after 1988.
figures.provenance: The 1967 Kernavė Rasa and the Ramuva movement's history are documented in Lithuanian ethnological literature and in Aitamurto & Simpson 2013; midsummer rites themselves are long attested in Baltic folklore collections.

figures.gloss: Ethnologist and founder (1939–2014): organized Ramuva in 1967, was barred from academic work in the Soviet crackdown of the early 1970s, re-founded the movement in 1988 and was consecrated krivis — chief priest — in 2002, taking the name Jaunius; heir, in the movement's self-telling, to the line of Vydūnas.
figures.facet: The founder as fieldworker: Trinkūnas built the revival out of his own ethnographic expeditions, which keeps Romuva unusually close to documented folk practice — and his consecration as krivis revived a title known chiefly from a medieval chronicle, the romantic claim and the sober method in one man.
figures.provenance: Documented biography (obituaries and Lithuanian press, 2014); Trinkūnas 1999; the krivis title from Peter von Dusburg's account of the Krive; scholarly treatment in Aitamurto & Simpson 2013.

figures.gloss: The household and holy fire personified, fed with bread and salt, 'bedded' each night by covering the embers in ash, and addressed in recorded Lithuanian folk prayers as Šventa Gabija ('Holy Gabija') — sung and invoked in Romuva rites distinctly from the fire's own kindling as an act.
figures.facet: Person and practice held apart on purpose: the amžina ugnis rite is the act of tending fire at the aukuras, while Gabija is the fire addressed as a living goddess in folk prayer — the roster previously held the rite but not the one being prayed to.
figures.provenance: Lithuanian folk prayers to Gabija recorded in 19th-20th century ethnographic collections; her place in Romuva liturgy documented in Jonas Trinkūnas (ed.), Of Gods and Holidays: The Baltic Heritage (1999).

figures.gloss: The Earth Mother as directly honored in Romuva ritual practice — libation poured and bread offered at rites, continuing the žemyneliavimas custom of thanking and feeding the earth recorded in 19th-century Lithuanian folklore.
figures.facet: A revival-side node distinct from the ancient goddess: the Baltic shard already holds Žemyna as the old-source deity, but Romuva's own libation practice is the living enactment of that cult across the same centuries-long break the movement's sacred fire also spans.
figures.provenance: The žemyneliavimas offering custom recorded in Lithuanian folklore collections; its continuation in Romuva ritual documented in Trinkūnas 1999 and in Michael Strmiska's studies of Baltic pagan revival.

figures.gloss: Prussian-Lithuanian philosopher and playwright (1868-1953), whose idealist writings on Lithuanian national spirituality and ethical self-cultivation the movement claims as its intellectual forerunner; named as honored precursor in the tradition's own period note.
figures.facet: The precursor without a node until now: Romuva's founder Jonas Trinkūnas explicitly assumed Vydūnas's mantle, so the roster's founder-figure previously stood without the very forerunner the tradition credits him with succeeding.
figures.provenance: Vydūnas's biography and philosophical corpus are documented in Lithuanian intellectual-history scholarship; his role as claimed forerunner discussed in Aitamurto & Simpson (eds.), Modern Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Central and Eastern Europe (2013).

figures.gloss: The autumn commemoration of the vėlės, the souls of the dead, kept with food, drink and fire left out or brought to the graveside as the year turns toward winter — observed in both living Lithuanian folk Catholic practice and Romuva's own calendar.
figures.facet: The ancestor axis of darna made concrete: Romuva's harmony-concept names the ancestors as one of the relationships requiring upkeep, and Vėlinės is the calendar rite that actually performs that upkeep rather than merely naming it as doctrine.
figures.provenance: Vėlinės as a Lithuanian folk-Catholic and pre-Christian commemoration of the dead is documented in Lithuanian ethnographic literature; its place in the Romuva calendar in Trinkūnas 1999 and Aitamurto & Simpson 2013.

edges.note: The revival-of seam at its cleanest: the dainos Romuva sings are the same collected corpus from which Saulė and the Baltic pantheon are known at all — the liturgy and the primary source are one body of song.

The rekindled sacred fire restores the attested cult-form of the old Baltic sanctuaries whose perpetual flame the chroniclers tied to the worship of Perkūnas — an attested rite revived across a six-century break, not an unbroken flame.

Class seam of European reconstructionism: each movement's modern systematization of an order-concept from old sources — darna as harmony to be maintained, wyrd as the web one weaves into — parallel constructions, no contact required.
Intra-tradition: the altar fire and the festival fire are the same instrument at two scales — the aukuras flame kindled at Rasa in 1967 is the founding case of the rite.

Class seam of neighboring folk-continuity revivals: Slavic Kupala and Baltic Rasa are independent midsummer fire-and-water festivals, each kept alive in village custom under other names and unmasked as each movement's flagship feast — parallel survival, not shared descent.

Class seam of Slavic and Baltic reconstructionism: both movements revive a priestly title straight out of a medieval chronicle's own language (volkhv from the Primary Chronicle, krivis from Peter von Dusburg's account) rather than from any unbroken succession of office-holders.

Intra-tradition: Gabija is the fire addressed as a living goddess in folk prayer; the amžina ugnis rite is the communal altar-fire practice that feeds and tends her — person and practice, one flame at two descriptions.

Romuva's libation and bread offering directly continues the žemyneliavimas custom the old folklore sources record — modern cult practice reaching back to the same Earth-Mother across the same revival break as the sacred fire.
Intra-tradition: Trinkūnas explicitly claimed Vydūnas's mantle as forerunner — the honored precursor and the founder, one claimed lineage of modern Lithuanian spirituality.

Darna names the ancestors among the relationships needing upkeep; Vėlinės is the calendar rite that performs that specific upkeep — concept and its enactment, not a proven doctrinal source for the festival itself.

Both fix the community's ritual year to a solstice turning-point with divinatory and luck-securing observances; a shared solstice-festival template with no claimed line of descent between Volga Chuvash and Baltic Balts.

dossier.essence: Romuva is the Lithuanian native-faith revival, founded as the Ramuva movement in Vilnius in 1967 by the ethnologist Jonas Trinkūnas, suppressed by Soviet authorities in 1971, re-founded in 1988 and registered as a religious community in 1992. It claims to restore the Baltic religion of Lithuania — the last country in Europe Christianized, in 1387 — working from a folk substrate unusually rich because the conversion came so late. The Seimas denied it state recognition in 2019; in 2021 the European Court of Human Rights found the denial a rights violation.
dossier.cosmos: The world Romuva inhabits is the one carried in Lithuanian folk song: Dievas the sky-father, Perkūnas the thunderer, Saulė the sun-mother, Žemyna the earth, Laima who allots each birth its lot. Its ordering concept is darna — harmony within oneself, the family, the ancestors and the living land — a modern systematization by Trinkūnas of folk material rather than an attested ancient doctrine. The dead become vėlės, shades who remain in relation with the living and are feasted at Vėlinės in early November; fire is the medium through which offerings and honor pass.
dossier.practice: Rites are sung, not read: communities gather around the amžina ugnis, the sacred fire kindled on the stone aukuras altar, feed it grain, bread, salt and beer, and carry the ritual in dainos and polyphonic sutartinės — the same folk hymns from which scholars reconstruct Baltic religion at all. The festival year centers on Rasa at midsummer, whose public celebration at Kernavė in 1967 — staged as ethnographic folklore because it could not be staged as religion — was the movement's founding act. Trinkūnas was consecrated krivis, chief priest, in 2002, reviving a title known chiefly from a medieval chronicle.
dossier.canon: There is no scripture: the liturgy is the folklore collections themselves, from Liudvikas Rėza's 'Dainos oder Litthauische Volkslieder' (1825) onward, with Trinkūnas's 'Of Gods and Holidays: The Baltic Heritage' (1999) as the movement's modern statement.
dossier.reflects: The rare case of a revival whose worship and whose evidence are one body of material — in singing the collected folk songs, Romuva performs its own bibliography.
sources: Jonas Trinkūnas (ed.), 'Of Gods and Holidays: The Baltic Heritage' (1999)
Kaarina Aitamurto & Scott Simpson (eds.), 'Modern Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Central and Eastern Europe' (2013)
Michael Strmiska, 'The Music of the Past in Modern Baltic Paganism' (2005)
Liudvikas Rėza (L. J. Rhesa), 'Dainos oder Litthauische Volkslieder' (1825)

links.cite: Liudvikas Rėza (L. J. Rhesa), 'Dainos oder Litthauische Volkslieder' (1825)
Kaarina Aitamurto & Scott Simpson (eds.), 'Modern Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Central and Eastern Europe' (2013)
